title: Guard arms the hard block on the 2nd trip OR when the nudge budget is exhausted
context: Spec §3 defines a literal two-strike: 1st trip nudges, 2nd trip denies. But the guard also has a nudge cap ('prevent nudge spam'). That leaves an unspecified case: a subagent that keeps tripping after its nudge budget is spent would otherwise get silence (no nudge, and not yet armed if we only arm on trip #2 counting). Surfaced while writing the B plan's decide_post tests — test_decide_post_respects_nudge_cap expects arming when the cap is reached. This is a safety-relevant gap in the literal two-strike.

options.name: arm-on-2nd-or-cap-exhausted
options.rationale: block_armed = (trips >= 2) OR (nudge budget exhausted). Once nudges can no longer fire, any further trip escalates straight to the PreToolUse deny.
options.tradeoff: Slightly more aggressive than the spec's literal wording; the guard can arm without a 2nd distinct trip if the cap was already spent.

options.name: literal-two-strike-only
options.rationale: Arm strictly on the 2nd trip; when the nudge cap is reached, simply stay silent and never escalate from that path.
options.tradeoff: Faithful to spec §3 wording, but a persistently rabbit-trailing subagent past its nudge budget gets neither nudge nor block — it can burn its whole budget/lease tripping into silence. A safety hole.

options.name: raise-cap-instead
options.rationale: Drop the nudge cap concept; nudge on every trip until the 2nd-trip block.
options.tradeoff: Removes nudge-spam protection the spec explicitly wants; floods the subagent context with repeated nudges.

reasoning: The guard's purpose is to stop unproductive looping with minimal nudge spam. Arming when the nudge budget is exhausted is a fail-safe: it guarantees a persistently-tripping subagent is eventually hard-blocked (and the worker then files a `blocked` result via sb block, ADR-deny→blocked) rather than left to burn quota in silence. In normal execution this is belt-and-suspenders — nudges only increment on trips, so reaching the cap (default 3) already implies trips>=2 and the block would arm anyway; the extra clause only matters in degenerate/contrived states, where it errs toward safety. It extends, rather than contradicts, the spec's two-strike intent (escalate when nudging isn't working).

steelman.strongest_case: Strict spec fidelity is valuable for a safety component: the spec author chose two-strike deliberately, and adding an implicit third trigger ('cap exhausted') is behavior the spec didn't sanction and a reviewer might not expect. In real runs nudges==cap already implies trips>=2, so the literal rule arms in every realistic case anyway — the extra clause is dead code that only complicates the invariant.

If the worry is silence-after-cap, the simplest cure is to not cap nudges at all — a looping agent keeps getting corrective feedback, which might actually break the loop without needing a hard block at all.

blast_radius: One conditional in decide_post (hooks/sb_guard.py). Affects only when the guard escalates to a PreToolUse deny; the deny path itself (and the downstream sb block) is unchanged. Tunable via guard_nudge_cap. Fully reversible to literal two-strike by dropping the `or not nudge_budget_left` clause. Confidence medium because the threshold defaults are first guesses (spec §7) and the real escalation cadence wants tuning against live guard-state data.
